imageCaption: A yellow door opens onto a street corner in Willemstad, Curaçao. Morning light catches on weathered colonial buildings. A morpho butterfly rests on the doorframe.
text:
The door had been yellow yesterday, but only theoretically. Today it was yellow in a way that committed. In a way that other yellow doors—and there were approximately five thousand of them in this block alone—recognized as legitimate membership in their architectural society.

Aaliyah stood on the threshold at 10:47 AM, trying to decide if opening it would unlock something in the house or if the house was just trying to tell her something about the neighborhood.

"You have to open it eventually," Elijah said, appearing over her shoulder with the confidence of someone who'd spent the morning exploring the seventeen bathroom configurations and had returned confident in his understanding of how houses communicated. "The house keeps making it brighter. It's like it's saying, 'Look at this, look at this, look at this—'"

"I understand the technique," Aaliyah said dryly. "It's not subtle."

But she pulled the door open anyway.

What came through wasn't just Caribbean air, though there was definitely that—salt and humidity and the smell of something cooking that made her mouth water in a way that suggested her entire body had immediately become invested in that smell's origin story. What came through was sound. Voices in Spanish and Dutch and what might have been English with an accent Aaliyah couldn't quite place. Music from at least three different buildings. The sound of dominoes slapping on a table. A child laughing in a way that suggested someone had just made a very good joke about something extremely silly.

And, underneath all of it, the sound of a house that had been waiting very patiently for its front door to open and introduce it to the neighborhood it lived in.

"That's loud," Luisana said, appearing in the kitchen doorway with her inevitable coffee. She was smiling though. The kind of smile that suggested she'd spent seventy-four days waiting to hear this exact sound in this exact combination. "In a good way. Very loud in a very good way."

"I'm going out," Elijah announced, and he was already moving past Aaliyah toward the street before anyone could construct a sentence containing the word "wait."

Sully followed, because Sully understood that where Elijah went, adventure followed, and adventure usually came with snacks.

Aaliyah looked at her parents. Anthony was already positioning himself to hang another license plate—he had a new one in his hand, this one Curaçao but newer, shinier, like it had just finished understanding what it was supposed to represent. Luisana had her journal open, pen poised, ready to document whatever the neighborhood was about to do.

"We can't just let him go into the street," Aaliyah said, but she was already following him anyway.

The street was narrower than it appeared from the doorway. The buildings on either side leaned toward each other like they were telling secrets, their yellow and pink and blue painted walls faded to a kind of soft pastel that suggested age and care in equal measure. There were windows everywhere, open windows specifically, which meant the entire neighborhood had just witnessed their arrival with the accuracy of people who understood that new arrivals were approximately as important as dominoes.

Elijah had stopped halfway down the block at what appeared to be a small colmadón—a corner shop—where an older man was arranging bottles of something that made Aaliyah's nose happy. The man looked up as Sully approached, and Aaliyah watched the exact moment where he shifted his expression from "dog approaching" to "dog that understands neighborhood protocol is approaching."

Sully sat. Politely. It was the most respectful greeting Aaliyah had ever seen him make.

"His name is Marco," the man said in English with an accent that placed him very firmly in this neighborhood for a very long time. "I am Marco. This dog understands respect. What is the dog's name?"

"Sully," Elijah said. "He's detecting things. Can I ask you something?"

"You are asking already," Marco said, but he reached down to hand Sully a small dried fish from the colmadón's shelf. "So yes. Ask."

"Does your street know it has a new house?" Elijah asked with the kind of seriousness usually reserved for significantly older people discussing very important things. "Because the house knows it has a new street, and it's very excited about it."

Marco laughed. It was the laugh of someone who had heard many strange things and who had learned that strange things were usually right about everything important.

"That house was empty four months," Marco said. "The owner wanted to sell, but the house said no. The house had opinions about timing. Now it has a family and it's excited about the street. Yes, this is how it works here. The house was waiting. We were all waiting. Sometimes you wait and nothing happens. Sometimes you wait and a family arrives with a dog that understands manners."

Rosa appeared then—Marco's daughter, Aaliyah would later learn, though she also worked at the university teaching something involving architecture and had opinions about restoration. She came out of the colmadón with two glasses of something cold that she handed to Aaliyah and Elijah without asking permission.

"You're in the yellow house," Rosa said. It wasn't a question. "I wondered when someone would open that door. The neighborhood's been very curious. Marco, they need to know about the street party."

"The street party?" Aaliyah repeated, but she was already understanding from the way Rosa's face had organized itself that this was important information, the kind of information that neighborhoods had and families needed to hear.

"Saturday," Rosa said. "Domino tournament. Cooking. Music. The entire block. Everyone brings something. You will come, yes? Your family will come?"

Aaliyah thought about asking permission. About running back to the house to check with her parents. About confirming that this was the kind of neighborhood activity that families who'd just traveled seventy-four days through temporal glitches were supposed to participate in.

Instead she heard herself say: "Yes. Definitely yes. What can we bring?"

"Bring something that comes from your family," Marco said. "Not a recipe. Something you invented. Something that only you would make. Something that tells us who you are."

And Aaliyah understood, standing in a street in Willemstad on what was technically the first official day of their lives in one place, that this was what it meant to put down roots. Not to stop moving. But to move slower. To let other people know you. To cook something that only your family would cook and bring it to dominoes on Saturday.

She looked back at the yellow door. Her mother was standing in it, coffee in hand, journal abandoned, just watching.

Their eyes met across the distance.

And Luisana smiled like she understood exactly what had just happened: the house had introduced the family to the neighborhood, and the neighborhood had said welcome, and nothing in their lives would ever be quite as loose or quite as uncertain again.

Which, Aaliyah realized, was exactly what they'd been running toward all along.

Saturday could not arrive soon enough.
